title: Reclaiming Practice After a Bad Teacher
dek: *When someone in a position of authority teaches you to override your body's wisdom, the path back to practice requires more than forgiveness. It demands a deliberate re-education of your nervous system, a patient untangling of what was true from what was imposed, and a willingness to let anger be the teacher it was always meant to be.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. A harmful teacher can make you distrust your own body's signals, but your capacity for felt experience remains intact beneath the conditioning. Reclaiming practice means separating the method from the messenger and rebuilding somatic trust through small, self-directed experiments. The anger you feel toward that teacher is often the first honest sensation to return, and it can become the doorway back into embodied awareness.

She sat on her mat for the first time in eleven months, cross-legged in the corner of her bedroom where the morning light came in sideways. Her shoulders were up near her ears. Her jaw was tight. She had set a timer for five minutes, thinking that would be safe, that nothing terrible could happen in five minutes. But thirty seconds in, her chest began to constrict, and she recognized the feeling: not panic, not grief, but a hot, coiled fury that had no name yet. She opened her eyes. The timer still had four and a half minutes to go. She turned it off and stood up, shaking, and realized she had been holding her breath the entire time. ## The Specific Harm of Authority Misused When a teacher abuses their position, whether through overt manipulation, boundary violations, or the subtler violence of spiritual bypassing, they don't just damage your trust in them. They damage your trust in the practice itself, and more insidiously, in your own capacity to feel what is real. In our experience, people who leave a harmful teacher often describe a kind of sensory amnesia: they can no longer tell the difference between relaxation and dissociation, between surrender and compliance, between devotion and fear. Peter Levine's work on trauma and the body emphasizes that when we cannot complete a defensive response (fight, flight, or protest), that incomplete action stays lodged in the nervous system. A controlling teacher often creates an environment where your instinct to say "no" or "this doesn't feel right" is pathologized as resistance, ego, or spiritual immaturity. You learn to override the very signals that would protect you. The practice becomes the site of the wounding. This is not about blame. It is about accuracy. You were taught to mistrust your own somatic intelligence, and now the work is to teach yourself again. That work begins with naming what happened, not in order to stay stuck in the story, but to locate the precise shape of the injury so you know what needs mending. ## Anger as the Return of Honest Sensation The anger that rises when you think about that teacher, or when you attempt to return to practice, is not a problem to be solved. It is information. It is your nervous system finally completing the "no" it could not say in real time. Over the years I've seen this pattern again and again: the clients who feel most ashamed of their anger are often the ones who were taught that anger itself was a spiritual failing, a sign of unfinished inner work, proof that they were not yet enlightened enough to understand. Gabor Maté writes extensively about how suppressed anger becomes internalized as shame, depression, or chronic illness. When you were in that teacher's orbit, your anger had nowhere to go. It turned inward. Now, as you consider re-engaging with practice, the anger is surfacing not because you are regressing, but because you are finally safe enough to feel it. This is progress, not pathology. Let the anger be there. Let it move through your body without commentary. Notice where it lives: in your jaw, your fists, the back of your neck, your solar plexus. This is not about indulging rage or lashing out. It is about allowing the sensation to complete its arc, to be felt fully so it does not have to keep looping. Anger, when it is allowed to move, often transforms into clarity, boundary, and a kind of clean energy that knows exactly what it will and will not tolerate. ## Separating Method from Messenger One of the most disorienting aspects of leaving a bad teacher is the confusion about what to keep. Do you throw out the entire tradition? The techniques? The language? The postures, the breathwork, the chants you once found beautiful? Some people need to step away from all of it for a while, and that is valid. But others find that the practice itself, when separated from the person who weaponized it, still holds value. This requires discernment, which is a capacity you rebuild slowly. Start by asking: what did I learn from that teacher that resonated with my own direct experience, before I started second-guessing myself? What practices felt nourishing in my body, independent of their approval or interpretation? What did I dismiss or override because they told me to? You might find that certain techniques were genuinely useful, but the context in which they were taught was coercive. You might discover that some of what you learned was accurate, but it was delivered with a subtext of control that made it impossible to receive cleanly. Pat Ogden's work in sensorimotor psychotherapy highlights how the body holds procedural memory: you can relearn a movement or a breathing pattern in a new context, and your nervous system will begin to code it differently. The same exhale that once signaled submission can become, in your own private practice, a tool for grounding and agency. You are allowed to take what serves you and leave the rest. You are allowed to rename things. You are allowed to practice in your own bedroom, with no one watching, with no doctrine, with [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) nearby and a cup of tea and the door locked. You are allowed to make it small, informal, and entirely yours. ## Rebuilding Somatic Trust Through Micro-Practices Trust is not rebuilt through grand gestures. It is rebuilt through tiny, repeated experiences of safety and choice. If sitting on a meditation cushion triggers a trauma response, don't sit on the cushion. If a certain breathwork pattern makes your chest tighten, don't do that breath. This is not avoidance; this is titration, a term Peter Levine uses to describe the careful dosing of sensation so that the nervous system can integrate without flooding. Here is a practice you can do in under five minutes, designed to restore your sense of agency: Stand or sit in a comfortable position. Place one hand on your heart and one on your belly. Take a slow breath in through your nose, and as you exhale, say out loud, "I can stop whenever I want." Repeat this five times, feeling the vibration of your voice in your chest. Notice what happens in your body when you give yourself explicit permission to stop. Notice if there is relief, or resistance, or disbelief. Do not try to change anything. Just notice. When you are ready, open your eyes and move on with your day. You have just practiced the most radical thing: choosing to stay present, and choosing to leave, on your own terms. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that the body is the ground of truth. Not someone else's interpretation of your body. Not a prescribed sequence of sensations you are supposed to feel. Your body, as it is, right now, with all its hesitations and refusals and quiet yeses. ## The Role of Supplemental Support Sometimes the nervous system needs biochemical support to downregulate enough for somatic work to even be possible. If you are stuck in a state of hypervigilance or chronic activation, no amount of breathwork will land until your system has some baseline capacity to rest. This is where targeted supplementation can be useful, not as a bypass, but as a bridge. [Natural Vitality Calm Magnesium Capsules (120 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BP4TQFC1?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help ease the physical tension that comes with unprocessed anger and nervous system dysregulation, particularly if you notice tight muscles, jaw clenching, or difficulty sleeping. Magnesium supports the parasympathetic nervous system, the branch responsible for rest and recovery. If your anger has turned inward and you are experiencing depressive symptoms or obsessive loops of thought, [NOW L-Tryptophan 1000 mg (60 tabs)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B002JNIAQW?tag=spankyspinola-20) may support serotonin production, which can stabilize mood without flattening the emotional range you need in order to process what happened. For those moments when the anger feels overwhelming or you notice a tendency toward bitterness or resentment that is starting to harden, [Bach Holly Flower Remedy (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005VW2I7K?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a gentle flower essence traditionally used to address feelings of hatred, envy, and suspicion. It does not suppress the anger; it softens the edges so you can work with it more skillfully. None of these are solutions. They are supports. The real work is in the repeated, embodied choice to feel what is true, to stop when you need to stop, and to begin again when you are ready. ## Finding New Models of Practice You do not need another guru. You do not need someone to tell you what your anger means or how quickly you should be over it. What you may need, eventually, is a model of practice that is non-hierarchical, transparent, and centered on your own authority. This might look like peer-led groups, body-based therapists who understand trauma, or solitary practice supported by books, recorded meditations, and your own trial and error. Some people find their way back through movement practices like yoga or qigong taught by instructors who emphasize consent and student autonomy. Others turn to expressive arts, to writing, to walking meditation, to anything that lets the body lead without a script. Some return to the tradition they left, but they do so with new boundaries and a willingness to walk out the door the moment something feels off. [The APA's research summary on anger management](https://www.apa.org/topics/anger/control) offers evidence-based approaches that honor anger as a valid emotion while providing tools for expression and regulation. The framework there can complement somatic practices, especially if you are rebuilding your capacity to set boundaries and communicate needs clearly. You might also explore the work shared on [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where the focus is less on dogma and more on direct, embodied encounter with what is. The companion journal you are reading now exists because we believe practice should never require you to abandon your discernment or override your instincts. If you want to learn more about the philosophy behind this approach, [the about page](/about) offers context on how these ideas took shape. ## When the Body Says "Not Yet" There will be days when you try to practice and your body simply refuses. Your breath stays shallow. Your mind scatters. Your shoulders lock. This is not failure. This is your nervous system saying, "I am not ready, and I will not pretend." Listen to that. Pat Ogden's work teaches us that the body has its own timeline, its own logic, and it will not be rushed. If you try to force yourself back into practice before your system feels safe, you will re-traumatize yourself. You will confirm the old belief that your needs do not matter, that you must push through, that surrender means submission. So when the body says "not yet," honor that. Go for a walk. Wash the dishes. Lie on the floor and do nothing. Let your practice be the practice of not practicing, of simply being in your body without an agenda. This is not laziness. This is deep respect for the intelligence of your own organism. In my own practice, I have learned that the times when I most want to push through resistance are the times when I most need to stop and ask what the resistance is protecting. Often, it is protecting a part of me that was not protected before. That part deserves to be heard, not overridden. ## Common Questions About Returning to Practice ### How long does it take to feel safe in practice again? There is no standard timeline. Some people return to practice within months, others take years, and some find that their relationship to practice is permanently altered. What matters is not the speed of your return, but the quality of your attention to your own readiness. Trust will rebuild at the pace your nervous system can integrate, not at the pace your mind thinks it should. ### What if I still feel angry at the teacher years later? Anger that persists is often anger that has not been fully felt or expressed. It does not mean you are stuck or bitter; it means there is still activation in your system that needs to move. Consider working with a somatic therapist, engaging in physical practices that allow for safe discharge (like hitting a pillow, vigorous exercise, or vocalization), or writing letters you never send. The goal is not to forgive or forget, but to complete the cycle so the anger is no longer running your life. ### Can I practice the same techniques I learned from that teacher, or do I need to start over completely? You can practice the same techniques if they feel genuinely nourishing in your body when you do them alone, without the teacher's voice or presence. The key is to notice: are you doing this because it feels good, or because you are trying to prove something, reclaim something, or avoid the grief of losing the practice entirely? If a technique triggers you, set it aside. If it feels neutral or supportive, you can keep it. You are the authority now. ## The Long Unfolding Recovery from a harmful teacher is not a linear process with a clear endpoint. It is a long unfolding, a gradual return to your own body, your own knowing, your own capacity to feel what is real without mediation or permission. There will be days when you are furious, days when you are numb, days when you try to practice and it feels like coming home, and days when the whole thing feels ruined and you wonder if you will ever trust again. You will. Not because you have forgiven or forgotten, but because you have learned to trust yourself. That is the practice now. Not the technique, not the posture, not the tradition. The practice is the ongoing choice to return to your own felt experience, to honor what your body knows, to stop when you need to stop and begin when you are ready. If you are looking for [more articles in this journal](/articles) that explore these themes, you will find a growing archive of perspectives on anger, embodiment, and the reclaiming of practice on your own terms. The teacher who harmed you may never acknowledge what they did. They may still be teaching, still be revered, still be causing harm. That is not your responsibility to fix. Your responsibility is to yourself, to the part of you that survived, that kept breathing, that is here now, reading these words, considering the possibility of return. That part of you is wiser than any doctrine, any guru, any system. That part of you knows the way back. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on reclaiming practice after a bad teacher, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
